title: Critical Thinking for Engineers
summary: Structured reasoning for technical decisions — recognizing cognitive biases, evaluating vendor claims and benchmarks, and staying analytical under the pressure of incidents and deadlines.

chapters.title: What Critical Thinking Is in Tech
chapters.summary: Critical thinking is not negativity or contrarianism — it's structured reasoning applied before acting. In engineering, where claims without evidence are common and decisions compound, it's a professional requirement.

chapters.title: Cognitive Biases That Bite Engineers
chapters.summary: Cognitive biases are systematic errors in reasoning that feel like good judgment. Engineers face specific high-stakes versions — confirmation bias in architecture decisions, sunk cost in legacy systems, planning fallacy in estimates.

chapters.title: Asking Better Questions and Evaluating Claims
chapters.summary: Engineering is full of claims — vendor benchmarks, "best practices," blog post architectures. Knowing what questions to ask before accepting any claim separates engineers who reason from evidence from those who cargo-cult from authority.

chapters.title: Critical Thinking in Practice
chapters.summary: Critical thinking applied to the specific situations engineers face every day — code review, incident response, sprint planning, design review, and hiring — where the pressure to act fast is highest and the cost of poor reasoning is highest.

chapters.title: AI-Era Critical Thinking
chapters.summary: LLMs produce confident, fluent, plausible-sounding output — which makes them dangerous for engineers who don't apply the same critical scrutiny to AI output as to any other technical claim.
